reasoning: Peer set (LEFT) is a dense dungeon/indoor tileset: desaturated grays, blacks, dark blues, teal accents, hard-stepped pixel shading, gritty stone textures, intricate small-feature density. Candidate (RIGHT) is a bright saturated green ground scatter with large loose pink/white flower blobs — warm, cheerful, soft-edged, low-contrast internal structure. (1) Palette overlap: near-zero; peer palette is cool-dark, candidate is warm-bright green+pink, no shared hue family. (2) Pixel density: peer tiles pack tiny deliberate features; candidate uses coarse scattered color blobs, far fewer distinct pixels per 32x32 equivalent. (3) Shading: peer uses classic hard-step shadows with clear outlines; candidate has gradient-like color blending between green base and flower petals — visually softer. (4) Warmth/saturation: peers are heavily desaturated cool; candidate is maximally warm and saturated — opposite end of spectrum. (5) Hand-pixeled charm: peers show intentional pixel placement; candidate looks algorithmically scattered or texture-filtered, lacking craft. (6) Photo-realism: low on both sides, not a disqualifying issue. (7) Overall feel: candidate reads as a bright casual/mobile game tile; peers read as dark dungeon-crawler. Even granting that outdoor vs indoor surface type is not penalized, every rendering-style dimension diverges catastrophically. This tile cannot coexist in the same game without a jarring visual seam.
